content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Pieces of April (2003) is a wonderful often overlooked Thanksgiving movie

**Score:** 77
**Body:** My wife recently brought up finding quality Thanksgiving movies to watch other then Plains, Trains & Automobiles. I remembered watching (and enjoying) Pieces of April about 20 years ago. I knew it revolved around Thanksgiving but the rest of the details were hazy. She had never heard of it so we ordered it off Amazon digital this afternoon and absolutely adored it. It's a really great, small budget dramedy with a solid handful of laugh-out-loud scenes. It's very well cast with a bunch of recognizable faces including a scene stealing Sean Hayes. I also found myself really enjoying the sound track. For a 20+ year old movie I think it really holds up well and was just as good if not better then I remembered. If you're looking for a sweet holiday Thanksgiving movie, check this out

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I haven't seen Planes, Trains...since it came out. Wonder if I should.
  - It’s a must not miss classic. Watch it
2. Patricia Clarkson is perfection.
3. Son in law is a thanksgiving go to for us
4. God I loved that movie. I had tried for years to find a way to describe the dynamic I had with my family and when she said “I’m the first pancake” I was like “yes! Exactly! There’s too much oil, the temperature is off you aren’t sure if you are undercooking it or overcooking it and it often just gets put to the side and abandoned.”
5. Katie Holmes
6. Stephin Merritt's soundtrack is 🔥🔥🔥
7. Me and my best friend loved this one in college.
8. May I recommend The Accidental Tourist?
9. I love that one.  Makes a great double-feature with Home For The Holidays with Holly Hunter and Robert Downey Jr, directed by Jodie Foster.

## 2. What are the best Vampire movies other than the ones I’ve watched?

**Score:** 55
**Body:** I’ve watched Only Lovers Left Alive, The Addiction, Sinners, all the Nosferatu films, Let Me In and Let The Right One In. Which Vampire films should I watch next?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interview with a Vampire is a classic. 
2. Try Daybreakers with Willem Defoe and Ethan Hawk. Truly different take on the genre.
3. Blade Trilogy
4. Bram Stoker's Dracula (1992)
5. Lost Boys
6. Near Dark
7. Not a film, but Midnight Mass is a good one
8. For a comedic take, What We Do In the Shadows. Personally I enjoy the film much more than the show.
9. 30 days of Night. Daybreakers. Bram Stokers Dracula.  Innocent Blood. The Hunger.  Abigail.  Lost Boys. From Dusk til Dawn.
10. 'Daughters of Darkness' (1971)

'Ganja and Hess' (1973)

'The Hunger' (1983)

## 3. Thanksgiving recommendation: Home for the Holidays

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Thanksgiving movie: Home for the Holidays (1995). Stars Holly Hunter and features Robert Downey, Jr. with Anne Bancroft, Dylan McDermott, Geraldine Chaplin, David Strathairn, and a young Claire Danes. 

Holly Hunter’s character gets fired from her job right before heading home for Thanksgiving, where family dysfunction, sibling rivalry, and drama are on full display — but there’s also caring and a bit of romance. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I personally like Mixed Nuts with Steve Martin, Madeline Kahn, Liev Schreiber, Adam Sandler, Juliette Lewis and more
  - I can’t find it streaming anywhere, darn it!
2. Planes, Trains, and Automobiles is the ultimate thanksgiving movie IMO but I'll check this out.
  - No doubt PTA is at the top - my second is watching Pauly Shore make a conservative small town lose its morals over Thanksgiving vacation with Son-In-Law and then the 80s family reunion slasher Blood Rage that has the greatest tagline of all time "It's Not Cranberry Sauce" and then the modern slasher Thanksgiving if you want to see Tim Dillon get beheaded.
3. Pieces of April [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pieces\_of\_April](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pieces_of_April)

Scent of a Woman [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scent\_of\_a\_Woman\_(1992\_film)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scent_of_a_Woman_(1992_film))
  - Pieces of April is wonderful.  A small slice of life type movie. I love it.
4. Thanks for these recs! I am going to thanksgiving with my 3 generation family and my elderly MIL is a big Hallmark movie fan, which grates on my nerves. I will have these suggestions in my back pocket, in case we get to choose something that will include some familiar actors and a followable fun storyline, that is not on the Hallmark Channel.
5. House of Yes. 

It's really best if you go into this one blind. 

It's a very dark comedy/drama.
6. It's kind of silly but Dutch is a great Thanksgiving movie. Like Planes, Trains and Automobiles but with Al Bundy instead.

I also recommend Rushmore.

## 4. Movies that are Small town Murder Mysteries and thrillers, psychological?

**Score:** 11
**Body:** hi all, i'm looking for good movie suggestions for Small Town murder / thriller/psychological mysteries? 

  
Usually they have a Sherriff, and small town cast. Often Horrors, like Scream, I know what you Did Last Summer, or History of Violence... 

Ideally  a whodunnit in a small town setting? 

thanks!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Red Rock West
  - Great call. Just started it again.
  - Fucking great film. Love this one. 
2. Cop Land (1997)
3. Misery 

The Wailing

The Wickerman (1973)

Weapons

The Crazies (2010)
4. In the Heat of the Night (1967)
5. Wind River is a must-see.
6. Silver Bullet

Stir of Echoes

The Dead Zone(1983)
7. Drowning Mona.
8. In the Heat of the Night (1967) -- a northern Black police detective helps a southern police chief to solve a murder in a small Mississippi town. An unusual film for its time, depicting the racism in a southern town, it portrays the main characters as three dimensional. A movie that is still relevant, exciting, and entertaining over 50 years later.

## 5. Please suggest some great Dramas, Love stories, emotional movies for my mom

**Score:** 12
**Body:** My dad recently passed away and I would like to find some great movies for my mom to watch to help district her. She loves Dramas, Love stories, emotional movies. Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated. Thank you. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moonstruck!
  - The answer is always Moonstruck
2. Local Hero
3. Sweet November is definitely an emotional movie and The Green Mile.
4. Second Hand Lions.   Emotional but does have some humor in death.
5. Some she might enjoy: The Notebook, Fried Green Tomatoes, Hope Floats, The Holiday, Ever After, Letters To Juliette, Momma Mia, The Lake House, Anna Kareninina, Ghost, Stardust, Cold Mountain, Tristan & Isolde, A Walk To Remember, Moulin Rouge, The Princess Bride, 50 First Dates or The Wedding Singer, Love Actually, Tender Mercies, At First Sight, Mermaids, Last of The Mochicans, Ladyhawke, Pearl Harbor. Sorry, so many more I could list.
6. P.S. I Love You

Caution, it’s about a husband that jJust died but a beautiful love story and how to get over loss
  - One of my go to movies when I need a good cry and smile.  
Also would add 50 First Dates and Leap Year.
7. Chances are (1989) - RDJ and Cybil Shepherd, about reincarnation and past loves. It's hard to explain but It's touching and sweet, yet light and funny. It's one i think of often then this topic comes up.  

Sorry for you loss. I lost my mom so I understand. Make sure to spend time with your mom and your with own thoughts and feelings in your own time.
8. CODA, One Chance, Crazy Rich Asians

The Man on the Moon 1991, The Gift, The Ultimate Gift

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are some other clearly made up stories about a movies production to generate interests?

**Score:** 2366

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. charlie sheen showed up as charlie sheen to do a scene then went home so he could do more charlie sheen.
  - Yea this one is definitely real lol. I wouldn’t be surprised if he was casted and written to be a handsome, flirty dude in that situation and he rolled up needing a bump and they just went with it lol
  - cocaine should have gotten a supporting the supporting actor screen credit.
2. I do not doubt Charlie is on the tail end of a 48 hour bender here.
  - He literally talks about it in his Netflix documentary.  It wasn't intentional, but his alarm went off as he went to bed.  And he was late.  But it wasn't an intentional 48-hours awake to play the part.  Charlie Sheen was just Charlie Sheen-ing.
3. He said he stayed up. He didn't say how.
4. Very similar, but Al Pacino’s bizarre acting in Heat was explained by himself as his character being a secret coke-addict. 

That, or Pacino was just high as a kite during production so tried to explain it this way 🤔
5. "Hannibal Lecter doesn't blink" is visibly untrue, but I can see how somebody started the rumor because he does blink less often than a human does naturally. Anthony Hopkins made multiple creepy acting choices.
6. Tobey McGuire doing the lunch tray stunt in camera with no special effects.  
7. The ghost of the kid behind a curtain on “Three Men and a Baby”.

## 2. What’s a movie quote you strongly disagree with?

**Score:** 983
**Body:** I’ll start. In The Karate Kid, Mr. Miyagi says to Daniel-San “No such thing as bad student only bad teacher”

That’s wrong because it implies only teachers can make mistakes. Both teachers and students can make mistakes; learning is a two-way street. 

The quote also overlooks the fact that students have free will. Teachers can try to teach their students moral values but the students end up making bad choices at times in spite 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Love means never having to say you're sorry."
  - good one, saying “sorry” is not a weakness - it’s respect. I’ve never understood why people romanticize that line.
  - That quote is basically a red flag wrapped in poetic wording.
2. I always took the point from Miyagi's quote was that the student's responsibility is to be willing to learn, and if they fail in that, then they're not really a student at all. It's the teacher's responsibility to teach their student - if their student is willing, then the result is within the teacher's gift and skillset to manage. It's also worth remembering this quote is specifically targeted at Danny - who is struggling with self belief and fear of his opponents, who are being trained by someone who's corrupted the martial art. Effectively, Miyagi is telling Danny to stop worrying about whether he can do it, what his opponent can do, and focus on his teachings.

I'd agree its not literally true though, even in the Karate Kid universe. Chozen is literally an example of a bad student as he had all the right training and direction but chose to misuse it, and its his remorse over his behaviour as a younger man that gets him on the right track in middle age.
3. “These aren’t the droids you’re looking for.”
Strongly disagree. They WERE the droids they were looking for.
4. "If you're not first, you're last"
  - That doesn't make any sense at all, you can be second, third, fourth... hell you can even be fifth
    - "What?!? I based my whole life on that"
    - I was probably high when I said that
5. Reading through this thread, you'd almost think it's a bad idea to get your philosophy/ethics from movies...

## 3. The Interview (2014): Funny or not?

**Score:** 677
**Body:** "He's no god! He has a butthole!"

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. YOU HONEDYDICKIN?
2. So funny that Kim John Un hacked Sony just so he could watch it before anyone else.
3. The Eminem opening part is pure comedy gold. I swear, everybody was talking about it after.
  - > Cleaning out his closet: finds himself

That scene had some hilarious headlines.
    - "Like I was leaving little breadcrumbs of gayness"
      - The real slim shady just stood up!
4. Eminem was the funniest out of everyone in the movie. Legit he needs to star in an all out comedy, he does deadpan so well.
  - Stephen Colbert:  You did the music for Southpaw with Jake Gyhllenhaal.  Does he always look that jacked?

Eminem:  They put his head on my body.
5. They hate us. Because they ain't us.
  - Because they anus?

## 4. One of the most underrated performers!

**Score:** 414
**Body:** Everyone constantly having conversations about the Emma Stone VS Jennifer Lawrence discourse but I honestly believe Amanda Seyfried clears most of the young actors today. She has quite the portfolio yet she is still so underrated!! 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not to mention she was the funniest part of Mean Girls.
  - Her boobs can tell when it’s raining
  - From Mean Girls we have her and Rachel McAdams who are both fantastic actors
2. Don’t forget her role in Jennifer’s Body!
  - We all loved a roll in Jennifers body
3. Love her !! 

Love her in every role.
4. “Wait, do I have Fuckme eyes?”

“No. You have, give us the Ring, My Precious, eyes”
5. Oscar nominated, emmy winning and highly praised doesn't scream 'underrated'.
6. don't forget Mamma Mia
7. yes, Amanda Seyfried deserves way more recognition than she gets. I think her performance as Elizabeth Holmes was incredible

## 5. Name a movie that references a particular date

**Score:** 219
**Body:** I want to compile a calendar of movies that make reference to specific dates in the hopes of watching said movies on those dates. Which ones come to mind?

Edit: Thanks to Redditor scd for telling me/us about ‎Dating the Movies: a list of films by Letterboxd. They have compiled a year's worth of films, some with overt references and others that are more subtle. Click 'read notes' to get the run down. https://share.google/rHwMuMMh6WRlvJ8As

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Miss congeniality. 

Describe your perfect date.

April 25th. Because it's not to hot and not to cold. All you need is a light jacket
2. V for vendetta.
3. Back to the future - november 5, 1955  
Back to the future part 2 - oktober 21 2015  
Back to the future part 3 - oktober 26 1985
  - Let us also not forget November 12, 1955
4. _Groundhog Day_: Groundhog day
  - Groundhog Day: Groundhog day
5. Terminator 2 - Judgement Day: August 29, 1997.
The day Skynet becomes self aware.
6. You are in the House of Elrond. And it’s 10 o’clock in the morning, on October the 24th, if you want to know.
7. Hot Fuzz (2006)


Oy! When's your birthday?


22nd of February.


What year?


Every year!
8. Remember, remember the 5th of November

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The joy of going to a crowded art-house film theater is overwhelming

**Score:** 201
**Body:** I was almost moved into tears. I discovered a local art-house which I've never gone to before and I had a smile the entire time. It was a small yet comfy venue. Posters of remastered versions of classic pictures or independent films or foreign films on the walls. Everybody inside was a film lover. I found my people. 

I got into the screening and if I'm blunt, I wasn't essentially enamored by the film I was watching ("O Ultimo Azul", Gabriel Mascaro), yet it was a old-styled picture house, carpet floor, though the screen was modern. 

I was surprised by the amount of attendees. It was a weekday afternoon yet the house was full. I often go to the multiplex and it's usually almost empty, except on weekends when you get families and loud teenagers. 

At the art-house, you saw varying ages and a genuine interest for the film. At the next door, they were showing Jafar Panahi's film and it was full. 

I was ecstatic. I already am excited to see the remastered Peeping Tom, by Michael Powell next. Possibly at night.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Funny enough I had a similar experience when discovering my local, when I went to see a showing of “Ainda Estou Aqui” but that film was also particularly phenomenal. The small theater was practically full, especially for the middle of the week, and surprisingly (or not) almost everyone was well over retirement age other than us and a few Brazilian couples
2. Completely agree. I'm very lucky to have a city with a decent arthouse theatre scene, and it's incredible how many people show up to some of the movies. I saw The Gold Rush in a packed theatre, and beforehand there was an introduction from a local Chaplin enthusiast and Charlie Chaplin's grandson:

"One hundred years ago, this film was shown in [a theatre a block away from where we were]. That theatre is now a bar, but The Gold Rush is still packin' em in."

A lot of people prefer watching at home, and that's fine. But I've had some unforgettable experiences at movie theatres, and I'll likely always prefer to see movies that way.
3. The best thing is going to see auteur driven/arthouse films in multiplexes - you often have the entire cinema to yourself. I very rarely go to mainstream films now, and certainly never at peak times, the audience cannot be trusted. Would rather just watch those at home. It's been really cool to see unique films like Bugonia and One Battle After Another go to number 1 in the cinema charts in the UK recently, seems folk are now sick of traditional mainstream fare.
4. My local art school has a theater, and it's become my favorite place in town. I've been there about 20 times in the past 3 years. I'm now a member and get discounts. My favorite movie I've seen there was The Hidden Fortress about 6 weeks ago.  I always tell myself I should go more often, and try to talk to people who clearly have a love for film like I do.  It's very different scene from my usual group of friends, in a good way. I've only gotten one person to go with me, and it was a date with a girl I should've held on to.
5. Me and most of my friends are members of our local art house. I went there last night, as they were the first theater in the area to show the new Knives Out film. Not an art house film obviously, but it really is so fun seeing movies in a packed theater full of fellow film geeks.
6. I've been lucky in being able to pass through Paris many times and whenever I do, it's one of my great pleasures to watch a film in one of their many amazing arthouse cinemas. Special shout-out to Le Champo and their retrospective on Ozu that made me fall in love with his films. Like you said, there is something so special about being in a place where everyone is digging it so hard. And when you leave the cinema, people kinda nod at each other like, yeah, we experienced something special together.
7. Cinemas like this are just so wonderful. I’m lucky to have a great local cinema chain (what you might call arthouse) in my city.

I haven’t even been for months (just personal reasons) but I am still paying my monthly subscription just because I would happily give them money to help keep them going. It’s one of the best things about the city I live in.
8. I often feel I'm part of something, even if I don't really talk to anyone.  I saw Eo (2022) at the Museum of Fine Arts in Houston.  It wasn't *absolutely* packed, but it was tight.  For whatever reason, I was so excited that almost a full house turned out to watch a weird movie about a donkey.   I went in blind, and loved it.
9. We have an awesome microcinema that concentrates in older repertory titles and indie and out-of-mainstream films, as well as a corporate ‘art’ theater that concentrates on those sort of “Oscar Bait for 50 year olds” films. 

The microcinema has had MULTIPLE sold out shows of *Holy Mountain* and *Stalker*, and was the first place in town to show *Hundreds of Beavers* and the ONLY place to show several sold out nights of *The People’s Joker*.
10. Watched Oldboy and in the mood for love in such a theater and what an experience both times. In the mood for love with a crowd feeling every missed opportunity. How beautiful it was on the big screen. Loved it much more in that setting than watching it alone at home

## 2. The moral or political message behind Bugonia?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I love how Emma’s character probes the various possible causes of CCD with Teddy and makes it very apparent that it can be due to other vectors unrelated to pesticides.

This is the actual ‘miscommunication’ we’re witnessing in the film imo. Whether it’s pesticides or something else, Bugonia wants us to see ourselves in Teddy. Fiercely appealing to hierarchy in assigning blame for whatever societal or personal ill we are experiencing. Be it a tech CEO, an Alien or a God, we never seek an answer which could be an ‘amalgamation’ of misunderstood lower level factors but that we demand a singular vector through which to filter our wrath and frustration.

We always seek to understand those above us in the hierarchy but it is NEVER in reverse. In the same way that the Aliens tried to ‘guide’ and preserve life to some extent in a “global” sense, they cannot understand the actual nuances of what our needs are nor do they care (Teddy’s mother). We can no more understand a tech CEO or a God than a Bee colony can understand Teddy's motives as a bee keeper.

I feel like the biggest statement this film makes is that blame seeking, EVEN IF you are completely right about your intuitions never results in a good solution. It’ll always end up as antifreeze in our veins. We as a society will always seek to blame and find restitution in higher powers / entities which cannot understand our grievances nor do they care about our individual suffering.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Your last paragraph reminds me of a quote that’s been bouncing around my head for a week that I heard from a 12-step recovery group member. “Expectations are resentments waiting to happen.” Make of that what you will involving Teddy kidnapping Michelle Fuller, or Michelle killing off the human race.
  - Indeed. I think that's at the heart of slave morality as well and what keeps us from progressing / finding solutions to our problems. Even if we find justification in our blame, the real solution is never going to be found in resentment.
2. I also found it funny how the cop character sought an understanding, wanted to know how Teddy felt all these years later after being abused by him as a babysitter. Even though Teddy was not overly concerned about what happened years ago at that point, the Cop's general interest in seeking a sit down, a dialog and some kind of forgiveness simply resulted is his own death however inadvertent. You can't really get forgiveness for abusing someone can you? You can't really get restitution from a chemical company for playing a role in your Mother's death "through communication", can you?
  - Forgiveness and restitution are two different things, though. I believe some people are more capable of forgiving than of allowing forgiveness for themselves. Part of me thought that Teddy forgave the cop because he was so all in on blaming the aliens for everything and another part had bigger fish to fry. Killing him with the bees was a pretty great compromise between practicality and revenge!
    - Yeah I agree. Or maybe it says more about communication never amounting to anything meaningful in reality. Forgiveness on the surface level of 'yeah don't worry about it man, I forgive you' doesn't actually mean anything at all. There are still consequences independent of who's blaming who. 

I dunno, lots of fun little moments that say a lot about communication that I thought were cool. 

There's something very psychologically deep about Yorgos' films that I've not quite seen from any other filmmaker. He just mines the absurd nuances of human behavior on the atomic level in ways that are both disturbing and true.
3. I saw it as a pretty straight forward class critique, though I'm probably missing layers. Teddy and Don are the have-nots of society. One is the seething impotent rage of the lower classes, the other a follower that enables the extremists by failing to adequately question or stand up to their peers until its too late. Their pain is real, their need for relief is real, but their inability to determine the actual causes for their situation leads to increasing disintegration of norms and devolves into violence.

Michelle on the other hand, represents the paternalism inherent to the upper classes. She's not the pinnacle of evils that she's made out to be, she even (initially) has benevolent aspirations to try to fix the problems of the world. But she does this out a vague sense of guilt, correcting a problem that started from the result of the aliens' meddling in Earth's development. She's not inherently good, and she doesn't really give a shit--as soon as she discovers the experiment has threatened those of her class (the other aliens) she's ready to abort the whole project and bail out. 

Teddy is fully impacted and invested in the problem but has no agency to actually figure out or solve it. Michelle has nigh-omnipotent agency to mess around with the human race but is only invested in it on a lark. Neither side is blameless nor intrinsically evil. One side creates local violence and mayhem, the other, coldly systemic violence, but in neither case is the problem truly solved.

## 3. Fuck AI, Guillermo del Toro said. Insights from a meeting with a production team of Frankenstein

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Hello fellow adults,

Many of us have been involved in Frankenstein discussions recently – what we liked, what we didn't, what was too much paint, and whether that was expensive paint or cheap Walmart stuff, or if there’s anything wrong with oscillating within the price range. Hoping to shed some light on the artistic choices – here are snippets from a meeting with the production crew of Frankenstein. Quoting whenever possible.

Part 1. Bringing the Monster to the Screen

Frankenstein was a movie that GdT wanted to make for years. An interaction between the Monster and the Creator. The time had come. It took 2.5 years to make – and it was his (auteur) adaptation of the book. Like Johnny Cash's version of Hurt, it was Guillermo del Toro's Frankenstein, whatever that may mean. The team was an army of artisans – painters, sculptors, every craft you can imagine. GdT and the team made use of them all.

Part 2. Time and Place

The art director treated Frankenstein as a period film. The "official" timeline for the movie was set 50 years after the 18-year-old Mary Shelley wrote her novel, which gave more freedom to the creators. The steampunk devices you can see on the screen were the actual mechanisms that would exist at the time of the Industrial Revolution. The chosen places were Wallace Tower in Stirling, Scotland, and the base for CGI was the coasts of Scotland and the UK. They would have people recreating the moss and making a puppet for Frankenstein, which was to "escape" the tower, as well as other scale models of buildings – to blow them up, of course.

Part 3. The Ship

It took some time to research, walking through drawings from the 1800s and real-life ship decks, and five months to build. It was actually erected in the parking lot for the team to be able to light it with a thousand cranes and telehandlers. The entire starboard side was completely open, with hydraulics and a giant engineering project – a million pounds of steel and crew access. The set was constantly updated to fit Guillermo's vision. “Can you add another 10 feet?” It was a logistical nightmare to shoot it in the parking lot, because all the crew and tech-truck parking was gone.

Every person in the art department drew a part of the ship. Devising the icicles was a separate huge endeavor, testing different silicones. They put it into Unreal Engine, which helped Guillermo visualize and get a feel for it. Their philosophy for digital work in general was to always start where they could with in-camera, practical, tactile stuff, and then build around it. The opening shot is digital and goes into the practical of the ship, then melts into the world. The scene feels seamless.

Part 4. The Real and the Fantastic

Guillermo liked to push into the fantastic, finding his ground in the realistic/historic sensibilities of the team, saying he wanted his team to be always 15% “pumped up.” It was a constant back and forth, like a dance. Take the costumes, for example – Victor is a scientist and also a dandy, so he's dressed in costumes inspired by David Bowie, Prince, and Jimi Hendrix. When he's strolling around like Mick Jagger – it’s intentional.

I know that there's a documentary on Netflix called Frankenstein: The Anatomy Lesson, but I haven't seen that yet. What do you guys think?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't get the connection between the title and the text, there's nothing about AI in what you wrote.


I'll admit that I haven't watched the film yet but I did watch the trailer, and it looks like a mix between a made-for-Netflix-original (and I mean that in a mean way) and a video game cutscene.


There are so few films that get the budget and freedom to actually do real practical effects, it's a crime against cinema to get that opportunity and then make it so ugly. 
  - This post feels like AI.
  - I feel like the actual camera angles and filmmaking part of the job is boring to Guillermo, he just chooses the props then goes home
  - I saw it at home and then paid to see it on the big screen, because it was one of the most beautiful films I’ve ever seen (in 6 decades). It was also deeply moving. Why are you commenting on a film you haven’t seen?
2. Who's idea was it to shoot it like Sky Captain and the World of Tomorrow? I thought it was cool how they did all that work and then made it look like a cologne ad filmed in two days in a volume
  - Omfg it's the best comment I saw on the film. It's really like Sky captain.
  - Come on that's unfair. There were shots in Sky Captain that were planned out and made with intention. Unlike here where the Steadicam just floats along like you're playing a bad Sony game
    - Sky Captain is a tremendous film and it pioneered a lot of the technology they use on stuff like the MCU films today :/

Kerry Conran is a huge part of why I wanted to become a filmmaker. He deserves way more respect than he gets, and it really makes me sad.
      - Haha, same. It was flesh over the bones of Metropolis, evolving into 1930s–1940s futurism and a dieselpunk aesthetic. I’ve got a full shelf of literature on retrofuturistic technology, weaponry, city planning, and all kinds of imaginative transportation – zeppelins, massive art-deco planes, flying cars with hydraulic wings, armored trains, giant submarines, even the occasional clunky retro rocket for spacefaring adventures. And the color palette – well, open any comic book from the ’40s and there it is. My little weird, bleak world of giant robots.

I’m okay with people disliking it, though. GdT called Skinamarink a movie of the future – and it was the only film in many years where I stormed out of the theater shouting that it was terrible. (It's like a guy filming the corners of his house and calling it a horror movie) Artistic disagreements happen. I just wish they were more informed. Know what you hate, and all that.
        - The fact that we never got the planned second film pains me to this day. What little I know of it involved the gang going to space and- from what I’ve read- the whole intro was going to be in black and white and then explode into glorious *Wizard of Oz* Technicolor once the rocket lifted off.

😭😭😭

## 4. Gene Hackman's Coach Scolds Robert Redford's Rookie in Downhill Racer

**Score:** 2
**Body:** A Scene from Downhill Racer starring Robert Redford & Gene Hackman  
An underappreciated film that deserves more attention than it received. It's a fantastic sports drama that uses the Downhill Racing sport only as a background and explores much deeper themes.

Sadly, we lost both these legends of acting this year. I loved this scene because they are both in their prime here in an [intense scene](https://youtu.be/036GPZtfX24?si=pV24llzvtb5op1z-)

## 5. Song recommendations for a Soundtrack inspired rave

**Score:** 4
**Body:** I’m a DJ and got invited to play at an indie short film festival that some friends of mine are organizing. I’m basically playing the afterparty and wanted to make a 2hr soundtrack inspired set mainly inspired by house / techno but with some other genres mixed in. Any recommendations? so far I got (in no particular order):

- Nightcall / Real Hero (Drive)
- Supernature / Dickmatized  (Climax)
- Basically the whole Challengers Score (Remix by Boys Noize)
- Love my way (Call me by your name)
- Woke up this morning (sopranos intro)
- Techno Syndrome (Mortal Kombat)
- Demon Dance (Neon Demon)
- Spybreak! (Playing God)
- In Motion (The Social Network)

Any more suggestions that kind of fit the vibe or any input in general will be greatly appreciated!!

1. Here's a few good scores/soundtracks to look into:

• Good Time / Uncut Gems (kinda ambient but very recognizable)

• Blade (late-90's techno)

• Run Lola Run (ditto)

• Fight Club (especially the opening track)

• Trainspotting (either of the Underworld tracks)

• Climax (or Irreversible or Enter the Void by the same director)

Also Clubbed To Death from the Matrix soundtrack would be iconic imo.

This is such a fun idea! Best of luck and I hope they love your selections
